name: B.S. in Business

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Eight parsed requirement groups lay out a clear four-year progression: a first-year foundation (ACCT 115, MGMT 190, MATH 135, ENGL 101), a functional core sequenced sensibly (accounting -> economics -> MGMT 116 quantitative analysis -> FIN 315 finance -> MRKT 330 marketing -> MIS/OM), business-concentration electives in the upper years, and a genuine capstone in MGMT 492 Business Policy that integrates the functional areas. Progression term over term is logical with no abrupt jumps.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit sophomore-level business-analytics bridge or a stated concentration-declaration point so students see how MGMT 216, MRKT 378, and MIS 445 chain into an analytics pathway.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible and appropriately shallow for a business degree (deepestPrereqChain 3), with reasonable grade conditions and OR-alternatives (e.g., ECON 265 'MATH 135 or MATH 138 or MATH 111', MGMT 216 'MGMT 116 or MATH 105'). The only dangling references are GER 200/GER 300 placeholders and MGMT 399 Career Planning, none of which are substantive prerequisite gaps.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the MGMT 399 Career Planning reference so it is not flagged as missing, and confirm concentration electives list their prerequisites explicitly on the plan.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data. Although the plan of study is strong, a catalog page for an AACSB-style business degree should state measurable program outcomes, and none are present.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes (e.g., analyze business problems with data, apply core functional-area knowledge, and communicate professional recommendations).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan is transparent and reasonably balanced across general education, business core, and electives, with per-term loads of 15-16 credits. However, statedTotalCredits is null and summedTermCredits is only 108 because several general-education items (History and Humanities GER, Natural Science GER) carry null credits, so the plan does not fully reconcile to a stated ~120-credit total. This is a data-consistency caveat rather than a curricular imbalance.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total degree credits and fill in the null credit values for the GER and Natural Science placeholders so the plan reconciles to the stated total.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page is well structured and legible: eight clearly labeled terms, per-term credit subtotals, and readable course titles for the required core. It is easy for a reader to follow the intended path. It falls short of exemplary because there is no program description captured and several GER credit values are blank.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short program description and complete the blank GER/Natural Science credit fields so every line on the plan shows credits.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required and elective courses reflect current business practice: MGMT 216 Business Data Analytics and MGMT 116 quantitative analysis in the core, plus electives in MRKT 378 Marketing Analytics, MRKT 360 Digital Marketing, MIS 385 Database Systems, and MIS 445 Decision Support Tools. Analytics and information systems are well represented. It is not exemplary because AI-for-business, sustainability/ESG, and fintech are not surfaced in the required core.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an AI-for-business elective (NJIT's MGMT 416 already exists) and a business-ethics/sustainability requirement to keep the core current.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled descriptions are present, specific, and informative (e.g., MGMT 492 as an integrative capstone across marketing/finance/operations/accounting, MRKT 378 on contemporary marketing analytics, MIS 445 on data-driven decision support). None read as thin placeholders; a few titles are abbreviated in the source.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally clear with sensible grade conditions and alternatives (e.g., MRKT 330 'MGMT 190 or MGMT 390 or departmental approval'). Minor blemishes exist: MGMT 316 has a 'Prerquisites' typo and a few marketing courses (MRKT 332, MRKT 433) merge prerequisite text into the description, which slightly reduces internal consistency.

missingTopics.topic: Business ethics and corporate social responsibility
missingTopics.rationale: The plan includes MGMT 290 Business Law but no explicit ethics/CSR course, a standard expectation for an accredited business degree.
The core has MGMT 216 and electives such as MRKT 378, but no required intermediate analytics or data-visualization course to build depth beyond the introductory level.
OM 375 covers operations broadly, but there is no dedicated supply-chain/logistics course, a core functional area in contemporary business programs.
No entrepreneurship course appears in the required plan even though NJIT offers ENTR 210/320/330; adding one as a concentration option would broaden the degree.
There is no coverage of sustainability, ESG reporting, or responsible-business strategy, now expected competencies for business graduates.

emergingTrends.rationale: NJIT already offers MGMT 416 Artificial Intelligence for Business Decisions; surfacing it as a business elective would connect the analytics core to generative-AI practice.
The presence of MGMT 216, MRKT 378, and MIS 445 positions the degree to formalize a data-analytics concentration that reflects data-driven management hiring demand.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital transformation and platform business models
emergingTrends.rationale: MGMT 480 Managing Technology and Innovation could anchor coverage of platform strategy and digital transformation that modern firms expect.
FIN 306 Blockchain Technology for Business exists in the catalog and could be offered to business majors as an emerging-technology elective.
Growing regulatory and investor emphasis on ESG makes a sustainability/ESG reporting elective an increasingly relevant addition to the business core.

topSuggestions: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes / educational objectives - none appear in the data.
State the total degree credits and fill in the null GER/Natural Science credit values so the plan reconciles (summed shows 108 with several blank items).
Resolve the MGMT 399 Career Planning dangling reference (on the plan but absent from the catalog) and the GER 200/300 placeholders.
Add an explicit business-ethics/CSR requirement and an AI-for-business elective (MGMT 416) to modernize the core.
Introduce sustainability/ESG and supply-chain options to round out functional-area coverage.
